segments.cost: free passage on the road itself; wagon, team, and supplies cost $50-$200 total for the full journey
segments.conditions: The first stretch followed established roads through the prosperous Pennsylvania Dutch farmland of Lancaster County. Families typically departed in spring after the last frost, joining other migrant groups heading south and west from the Philadelphia hinterland.

segments.cost: included in journey provisions; ferriage across the Susquehanna River cost a few pence
segments.conditions: The road crossed the Susquehanna River and passed through York and the Maryland panhandle before entering the Great Valley of Virginia. Scots-Irish and German families traveled in loose convoys of 5-15 wagons for mutual protection, with livestock driven alongside.

segments.cost: forage and occasional tavern stays; many families camped in the open
segments.conditions: The Shenandoah Valley corridor was the heart of the Great Wagon Road — a broad, fertile valley flanked by the Blue Ridge and Allegheny ranges. The road followed old Iroquois and Cherokee trading paths, and settlers encountered both friendly trade and growing tension with Native peoples whose land they were entering.

segments.to: Moravian settlements / Salisbury, North Carolina (Carolina Piedmont)
segments.travel_time: 5-7 days by wagon through mountain gaps
segments.conditions: Crossing through the gaps in the Blue Ridge into the Carolina Piedmont required navigating steep, rocky paths barely wide enough for a wagon. The Moravian settlements around Salem (modern Winston-Salem) served as a critical waypoint, offering provisions, blacksmithing, and news of conditions further south.

segments.cost: included in journey provisions; some families stopped permanently along this stretch
segments.conditions: The southern stretches of the road passed through increasingly isolated backcountry where European settlement was sparse and Cherokee territory began in earnest. Many families never reached Augusta — they chose promising creek bottoms or ridge sites along the way and simply stopped, becoming the founding settlers of communities across the Carolina and Georgia Piedmont.

who_used_it: Scots-Irish (Ulster Scots) and German Palatinate settlers moving south and west from Pennsylvania into the Shenandoah Valley, Carolina Piedmont, and eventually into Kentucky and Tennessee through the Cumberland Gap. This single route accounts for the settlement of much of the southern backcountry between 1740 and 1810.
narrative_hook: In {year}, {ancestor_name} likely followed the Great Wagon Road south from {origin} through the Shenandoah Valley toward {destination}, traveling 4 to 6 weeks by wagon along a route that moved more settlers into the southern backcountry than any other path in colonial America.
source_references: Parke Rouse Jr., 'The Great Wagon Road: From Philadelphia to the South' (Dietz Press, 1973)
David Hackett Fischer, 'Albion's Seed: Four British Folkways in America' (Oxford University Press, 1989)
Warren R. Hofstra, 'The Planting of New Virginia: Settlement and Landscape in the Shenandoah Valley' (Johns Hopkins University Press, 2004)
Carl Bridenbaugh, 'Myths and Realities: Societies of the Colonial South' (Louisiana State University Press, 1952)
